history: The client has a longstanding history of family conflict, particularly with a dismissive and unsupportive father, leading to estrangement. There is ongoing stress related to the partner's family, especially a difficult relationship with the partner's sister-in-law. The client finds satisfaction and a sense of control in academic and teaching pursuits, often using these as a refuge from emotionally charged family dynamics. The client describes feeling empowered in structured intellectual environments but inadequate and frustrated in personal relationships.
unlovable_belief: I am bound to be abandoned
I am bound to be rejected
intermediate_belief: If I assert myself or set boundaries, others will dismiss or reject me, just like my father did. I need to avoid confrontation to prevent being hurt or excluded. If I can't make things better, it means I'm failing as a partner and family member.
intermediate_belief_depression: If I try to set boundaries and it doesn't work, it will just prove that I'm incapable of having healthy relationships and nothing will ever change. There's no point in trying because it always ends up the same.
coping_strategies: The client immerses themselves in academic and teaching activities to gain a sense of mastery and avoid dealing with interpersonal conflict. They withdraw emotionally from challenging family situations and focus on intellectual pursuits as a distraction from distress. This avoidance prevents them from addressing the core issues with their partner's sister-in-law.
situation: Deciding how to address rude behavior from the partner's sister-in-law and whether to set boundaries.
auto_thought: If I try to stand up to her, she'll just ignore me or make things worse. I can't handle another conflict like with my dad.
behavior: I avoid bringing up the issue with my partner's sister-in-law. Instead, I distract myself by focusing on work or teaching. I keep my feelings to myself and don't set any boundaries.
